water_temp_read.read: Water temperature is in a favorable trout range.

light_pressure_read.read: Light and pressure do not strongly push the outlook either way.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

generated_text.quick_read: The Chattahoochee River is offering very good trout fishing conditions with an ideal water temperature of 57.9°F and moderate flow. Recent stocking supports using stocked-trout attractors along with natural nymph patterns.
generated_text.best_window: Day 1 morning is best due to moderate sky cover, cool air temperatures, and steady flow, creating comfortable feeding lanes where trout actively feed in riffles, runs, seams, and near structure.
generated_text.current_conditions: Water temperature is 57.9°F, flow is 933 cfs, gage height is 2.01 ft, and turbidity measures 6 NTU.
generated_text.weather_setup: Sky cover averages around 46% in the morning with light rain probability and no expected rain. Pressure is 30.05 inHg with an unknown trend. Winds average 5.3 mph with gusts up to 15 mph, and conditions are stable for trout activity.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout favor comfortable feeding lanes close to cover in riffles, runs, seams, and structure. Use clean drift presentations with smaller natural flies like pheasant tail and zebra midge, alongside stocked-trout attractors like egg pattern, squirmy worm, and duracell. Focus on nymphs, midges, soft hackles, and balanced tactics while watching for subtle changes in trout behavior.
